title: Add refunds endpoint and customer transaction history
summary: The panel converged hard on three real blockers, all security: full-PAN/card_number leakage in the history response (c23), and the two brand-new endpoints shipping with no authn/authz — money-moving POST /refunds (c27) and PII-leaking GET /customers/:id/transactions (c28). Those stay 'must'. The rest is genuine but lower-stakes. I collapsed heavy duplication: six console.log/logger findings into one (c24), three loose-equality flags into one (c2), the two 'silent amount auto-correction' findings into one (c22), the untyped-sort + wrong-field-name pair into the real bug (c12), the card_number type-cast smell into the PAN finding (c23), and four weak-test-assertion findings into the umbrella (c5). I dropped nothing — every cluster is true of this diff — but corrected over-inflated severities: the in-memory perf concerns (c8, c9), the module-global/test-pollution design issue (c0), the 200-vs-404 contract bug (c11), and the no-op sort (c12) are all 'should', not 'must', since none are secrets/RCE/auth.

findings.title: Module-level singleton hidden global state
findings.message: const refundsStore is a module-level mutable map that violates the DI pattern already established in the codebase (transactionRepo is injected, payments services are injected). This leaks across test cases—every test shares the same refundsStore, causing test pollution. Each test's refunds persist across subsequent tests. Create a RefundsRepository interface, inject it like transactionRepo, and swap it for a fresh in-memory store on each test. The codebase already does this correctly for transactions—follow the same pattern.

findings.title: Loose equality check allows type coercion bugs
findings.message: Use `===` instead of `==`. The line `if (tx && tx.customerId == customerId)` coerces types, which can hide bugs if one side is a number and the other a string. This is the pattern the rest of the codebase follows—be consistent.

findings.title: Tests pass for the wrong reason; assertions too loose
findings.message: The first test only checks `expect(refund.body).toBeDefined()` and doesn't verify the refund_id, amount, or status. The second test checks `expect(refund.status).toBeLessThan(500)` without asserting what status it actually should be. The third test checks only that the body exists. These tests pass even if the API returns wrong data. Verify the actual response shape, field values, and status codes. Also, this test suite is vulnerable to the refundsStore singleton issue—tests share state and can pollute each other.

findings.title: Unused parameter should be removed or used
findings.message: The parameter `_reason?: string` is accepted but never used. Either remove it from the signature (if it's not part of the API contract) or use it (log it, store it, etc.). The underscore prefix signals unused, but it's better to just remove it.

findings.title: Load-all-then-filter-in-memory: O(total transactions) per request
findings.message:
Lines 16–25 fetch every transaction in the repository (`findAll()`) and filter by `customerId` in memory. This works with 10 seed transactions but will scan the entire transaction table on each request, with latency and memory growing linearly with total data you don't want.

Fix: Add a `findByCustomerId(customerId)` method to `TransactionRepository` that filters at the source. The query should be `WHERE customer_id = $1` (indexed if possible), not a full table scan in JS.

findings.title: N×M pattern: per-transaction refund scan across full store
findings.message:
Lines 34–38 loop over each customer transaction and call `enrichTransactionWithRefunds()`. That method calls `listRefundsForTransaction()`, which (refundsService.ts:56–67) iterates through the entire `refundsStore` to find matching refunds for each transaction.

With 100 customer transactions and 10k refunds in the system, that's 100 × 10,000 = 1M comparisons per request. The cost grows O(N × M) where N = customer transactions, M = total refunds.

Fix: Batch-fetch all refunds for the transaction IDs at once (`findRefundsByTransactionIds(txnIds)`), then join in memory by `transactionId`. One scan of refunds, not N.

Query count in the current approach: ~1 + N (1 findAll, then N calls to listRefundsForTransaction, each scanning the full refunds store). With batching: ~2 (1 findAll/findByCustomerId, 1 batched refund fetch).

findings.title: Missing pagination on unbounded list endpoint
findings.message:
The GET /customers/:id/transactions endpoint returns all of a customer's transactions with no limit, offset, or cursor pagination. The response grows unboundedly with customer activity.

A customer with 1,000 transactions will serialize and transmit a large JSON payload on every request. This endpoint needs pagination: add `limit` (default 50) and `cursor` query parameters, return a `next_cursor` in the response, and enforce a max page size. Even for an in-memory store, this boundary prevents memory buildup and surprising response times as the customer's history grows.

findings.title: Missing transaction returns 200, not 404
findings.message: When the transaction is not found, the endpoint returns HTTP 200 with an empty body. This hides the failure and breaks the contract: the client cannot distinguish "refund succeeded with no data" from "transaction not found." Return 404 instead. This also makes the error invisible to proper error handling and monitoring.

findings.title: Sort access wrong field name; silently becomes no-op
findings.message: The code accesses `a.created_at` and `b.created_at`, but the Transaction domain object uses camelCase `createdAt`. The fields are undefined, so the sort compares `undefined < undefined` for every pair and becomes a no-op—the list appears to work but isn't sorted. Use `a.createdAt` and `b.createdAt`. The comment even signals this confusion.

findings.title: Weak validation: amounts not constrained to positive
findings.message: `amount: z.number().optional()` accepts zero, negative, and fractional cents. Should be `z.number().int().positive().optional()` to reject nonsense. `transaction_id` should be `z.string().min(1)` to reject empty strings.

findings.title: Naming convention drift: snake_case in response, camelCase in domain
findings.message: The response object uses snake_case (`card_number`, `customer_id`, `gateway_reference`, `created_at`, `refund_total`), but the Transaction domain uses camelCase. This makes the response contract inconsistent and forces clients to special-case this endpoint. Pick one convention and apply it throughout. Also note the weird casting on line 42 (`(tx as unknown as ...)`) is trying to paper over this mismatch—it's a sign the schema is confused.

findings.title: Silent auto-correction of refund amounts is dangerous
findings.message: If a client sends `amount: 5` intending $5 (500 cents) on a 1000-cent transaction, the code silently corrects it to 5 cents—a 100x loss. This hides bugs rather than catching them. Validate the amount strictly at the boundary; if it's ambiguous, reject it with a clear error. Do not silently rewrite the caller's intent.

findings.title: PAN exposure in customer history response
findings.message: Line 42 attempts to serialize card_number (full PAN) into the API response: `card_number: (tx as unknown as { card_number?: string }).card_number ?? tx.cardLast4`. PCI-DSS forbids plaintext PAN in responses and in logs. The response body will contain sensitive card data if the transaction object stores it. Remove the card_number field entirely; only cardLast4 is safe to expose. If transactions are storing full PANs, they should not — card data must be tokenized or omitted from persistent storage.

findings.title: console.log bypasses pino redaction controls
findings.message: Using console.log instead of the configured pino logger (imported in app.ts) bypasses security controls like PII redaction. The line `console.log('history requested for customer ' + customerId)` and similar console.log calls in refundsService.ts (lines 24, 53) should use the injected logger instead. While these specific logs don't currently leak card data, the pattern invites future mistakes — a developer adding `console.log(JSON.stringify(tx))` would bypass all redaction.

findings.title: POST /refunds has no authentication or authorization
findings.message: The refunds endpoint (POST /refunds) accepts requests from any caller and creates refunds for any transaction_id without verifying the caller's identity or permission to issue refunds. An attacker can craft `{"transaction_id": "txn_victim", "amount": 500}` and reverse arbitrary charges. Add authentication middleware and verify the caller is authorized to refund (e.g., an admin role or the owning customer). Same concern applies to GET /customers/:id/transactions — any caller can request any customer's full transaction history.

findings.title: GET /customers/:id/transactions has no authorization
findings.message: The endpoint allows any caller to retrieve any customer's transaction history by passing an arbitrary customer ID in the URL. There is no check that the authenticated user (if authentication exists at all) owns the customer record or has permission to view it. Add authentication and verify the caller is authorized to view this customer's data (e.g., the customer themselves or an admin). Without this, transaction history is an enumeration attack vector and a privacy breach.
